coredata.dc:title: Visible-light-driven pollutants degradation with carbon quantum dots/N-TiO2 under mild condition: Facile preparation, dramatic performance and deep mechanism insight
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Jinteng
Cao, Yuehong
Zhao, Peng
Xie, Tengfeng
Lin, Yanhong
Mu, Zhao
coredata.dc:description: Because pure TiO2 has high photogenerated charge carriers recombination rate and inferior visible light capture capability, the optical activity is very weak. N doping can generate a lower energy gap energy and carbon quantum dots (CDs) have excellent properties that can transfer electrons smoothly and reserve electrons quickly, so we think that doping of N into TiO2 and loading of CDs can modify TiO2. We synthesized a brand new carbon quantum dots/N-TiO2(CDs/N-TiO2) by mechanically mixing carbon quantum dots with N-TiO2 instead of the conventional calcination or solvothermal step. The degradation ratio of RhB(10 mg/L) was increased from 6.66 % and 19.97 %–99.8 % in 5 min under visible light when compared with TiO2 and N-TiO2. The kinetic constants of RhB degradation by 30CDs/N-TiO2 are 85.52 and 25.40 times higher than those of TiO2 and N-TiO2, respectively. Besides, Cr(Ⅵ) (20 mg/L) photoreduction can be completely achieved in 4 min under solar light irradiation. Besides, We analyzed the behavior of photogenerated charge carriers by the surface photovoltage spectra (SPV) and transient photovoltage spectra (TPV) measurement analyses, showing the deep mechanism of photodegradation process of dye. Doping of nitrogen into TiO2 can extend the photo absorption range of TiO2 and let n-type TiO2 show p-type conducting character that makes it easier to transfer electrons to the CDs. Loading of CDs can enhance the electron-hole pairs separation efficiency.

head.ce:title: Visible-light-driven pollutants degradation with carbon quantum dots/N-TiO2 under mild condition: Facile preparation, dramatic performance and deep mechanism insight

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry, Jilin University, Changchun 130012, People’s Republic of China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry, Jilin University, Changchun 130012, People’s Republic of

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Engineering, Beijing Institute of Petrochemical Technology, Beijing 102617, People’s Republic of China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: ·School of Chemical Engineering, Beijing Institute of Petrochemical Technology, Beijing 102617, People’s Republic of China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Because pure TiO2 has high photogenerated charge carriers recombination rate and inferior visible light capture capability, the optical activity is very weak. N doping can generate a lower energy gap energy and carbon quantum dots (CDs) have excellent properties that can transfer electrons smoothly and reserve electrons quickly, so we think that doping of N into TiO2 and loading of CDs can modify TiO2. We synthesized a brand new carbon quantum dots/N-TiO2(CDs/N-TiO2) by mechanically mixing carbon quantum dots with N-TiO2 instead of the conventional calcination or solvothermal step. The degradation ratio of RhB(10 mg/L) was increased from 6.66 % and 19.97 %–99.8 % in 5 min under visible light when compared with TiO2 and N-TiO2. The kinetic constants of RhB degradation by 30CDs/N-TiO2 are 85.52 and 25.40 times higher than those of TiO2 and N-TiO2, respectively. Besides, Cr(Ⅵ) (20 mg/L) photoreduction can be completely achieved in 4 min under solar light irradiation. Besides, We analyzed the behavior of photogenerated charge carriers by the surface photovoltage spectra (SPV) and transient photovoltage spectra (TPV) measurement analyses, showing the deep mechanism of photodegradation process of dye. Doping of nitrogen into TiO2 can extend the photo absorption range of TiO2 and let n-type TiO2 show p-type conducting character that makes it easier to transfer electrons to the CDs. Loading of CDs can enhance the electron-hole pairs separation efficiency.

ce:section.ce:para: It is a growing problem of organic contaminants in dyestuff industry with the huge industrial progress over the years, on account of because of the stable physical and chemical properties, and the difficult degradation by nature or conventional means [1–5]. As a result, new approaches for the degradation of RhB in the sewage are called for. In a lot of ways, the photocatalytic degradation method, particularly visible light photocatalytic degradation of RhB, has attracted a lot of attentions on account of its low cost, extraordinary activity, and the great capacity of solar power [6]. Nowadays, many novel photocatalysts have been developed. Yan et al. have prepared many biomimetic photocatalysts that have superior photocatalytic activity by self-assembly method [7–10]. Although novel photocatalysts are being developed continuously, traditional photocatalysts are still playing important roles due to their unique advantages. Not only its great catalytic activity and high chemical stability, but also its low toxicity and low price compared with other photocatalysts, titanium dioxid (TiO2) has attracted significant attention [11–14]. However, pure TiO2 has high photogenerated charge carriers recombination rate and inferior visible light capture capability [15,16]. So the meaning of developing the super photocatalytic activity of TiO2 is significant. To achieve this goal, researchers used many means to modify TiO2. Zhang et al. prepared a novel graphene-like TiO2@C nanocomposite to remove methylene blue and Pb2+ [17]. Yan et al. modified the surface of TiO2 with polydopamine to improve its photocatalytic performance [18]. Doping other elements into TiO2 has been considered as a resultful method [19–21]. Not only the low ionization energy, but also that the atomic size is similar with oxygen, N doping has been paid much attention by researchers [22]. Because N doping can broaden the optical responsive range to visible light for the reason of that a lower energy gap is generated [23–25]. Sometimes, N doping can let n-type TiO2 show p-type conducting character that makes it easier to transfer electrons to the CDs [26].

Carbon quantum dots (CDs) that were discovered recently not only had outstanding up-conversion photoluminescence characteristic, but alao had the excellent properties of transferring and reserving electrons, all of which insured the catalysts to take advantage of the sunlight efficiently [27–30]. By reason of these noteworthy characteristics, many photocatalysts were combined with CDs to promote photocatalytic performance. Di et al. reported an efficient CQD/Bi2MoO6 nanocomposite that had excellent photocatalytic performance in ciprofloxacin photodegradation than pure Bi2MoO6 [31], but the fabrication process of this nanocomposite was complicated and it takes 120 min to remove 88 % of ciprofloxacin under visible lilght. Besides, they didn’t give a deep insight into the mechanism. Li et al. prepared the Ag+-CDs-Bi2WO6 that showed outstanding activity in photodegradation of TC than pure Bi2WO6 by conventional hydrothermal step. However, it also takes 20 min to photodegrade TC (20 mg/L) completely and the dynamic performance of charge carriers was not explored [32].

Although there have been some reports of photocatalysts combining CDs with TiO2, however, the complicated conventional calcination or hydrothermal method were used to combine these two nanocomposites and the photodegradation time of pollutants was too long. Besides, the mechanism of the degradation process has not been further explored. In this paper, we reported a easy means to prapare the N-TiO2 and a very simple mechanical mixing method instead of conventional calcination or hydrothermal step to. Although the synthesis process was the simple mechanical mixing method, the stability of the CDs/N-TiO2 was excellent that the RhB photodegradation rate still maintained at ca. 94.1 % after five recycling experiments. We used varioius instruments to explore the characters of CDs/N-TiO2 including the transient photovoltage (TPV) to reveal the dynamic performance of electrons or holes, which shows the deep mechanism of photodegradation process of dye. The photocatalytic performance of the photocatalysts was evaluated by photodegradation of RhB and Cr(Ⅵ) and only a few minutes were needed to remove these pollutants completely. Doping of nitrogen into TiO2 can can broaden the optical responsive range to visible light and let n-type TiO2 show p-type conducting character that makes it easier to transfer electrons to the CDs. Loading of CDs can enhance the electron-hole pairs separation efficiency.

Urea ((NH2)2CO, Beijing Chemical Works), Ethanol (C2H6O, Beijing Chemical Works), Tetrabutyl titanate ([CH3(CH2)3O]4Ti, Tianjin Guangfu Fine Chemical Research Institute), l-Ascorbic acid (C6H8O6, Beijing Chemical Works). Redistilled water was prepared in our laboratory.

After adding 0.25 g of ascorbic acid into 59 mL of ethanol, then we added 20 mL of redistilled water into the solid-liquid mixture. Before transferred the mixture to the high-pressure reactor for reaction at 160℃ for 3 h, the mixture was stirred for 10 min. After natural cooling, it was put into the environment at 5 ℃ for preservation [33].

ce:section.ce:section-title: preparation of TiO2 gel and TiO2
ce:section.ce:para: First, sol-gel method synthesizing of TiO2 xerogel: 4 mL of tetrabutyl titanate (TBOT) and 2 mL of anhydrous ethanol were mixed under continuous agitation, then mixed half hour. The mixed solution of 17 mL of anhydrous ethanol, 1 mL of nitric acid and 0.4 mL of secondary distillation water was mixed with the above mixture. The transparent sol was gotten after continuous agitation for several hours, and then dried it for 6 h under 110 ℃. TiO2 nanoparticles were obtained by calcining the TiO2 xerogel for 6 h under 400 ℃.

First, urea and TiO2 xerogel by mass ratio 3:1 were mixed by grinding, and ultrasonic dispersion was carried out after joining the right amount of water into the mixture. After the above processes were completed, the suspension was put into a high pressure reactor and reacted for 10 h under 240 ℃, and then dried under 110 ℃. Finally, a certain amount of nitrogen doping TiO2 nanoparticles were obtained after calcined for 6 h under 200 ℃.

First, the mixture containing 100 mg of N-TiO2 and 50 mL of anhydrous ethanol was gotten by agitation, and then sonicated it for 15 min. 10, 30, or 50 μL of CDs solution were added into above solution respectively under continuous agitation and stirred for 1 h. Before drying at 90℃ for a night to get the final products, the products were centrifuged and washed many times with distilled water. Meanwhile, we prepared the CDs/TiO2 nanocomposite by same method.

The crystalline phase was detected by X-ray diffraction (XRD) on a Rigaku D/Max-2550 diffractometer using Cu Ka1 radiation (λ =1.54056 Å). The scanning electron microscopy (SEM) system (Hitachi, Model S-4800) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (FEI Tecnai G2S-Twin) with a field emission gun carrying out at 200 kV were used to study the morphology, size, and elemental distribution of the photocatalysts. The diameters of the photocatalysts were detected by Image visualization software (Image J). The nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherm measurement (Micromeritics ASAP 2020 HD88, USA) was used to characterize the Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) surface areas of the photocatalysts. UV–vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, UV-3600) was used to characterize the UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra (UV–vis DRS) of nanocomposites over the range of 300–800 nm. The sample cell, a computer, a lock-in amplifier (SR830-DSP) with a light chopper (SR540), and a source of monochromatic light make up the lock-in based surface photovoltage (SPV) equipment. The monochromatic light was provided by a 500 W xenon lamp and a grating monochromator (Omni-λ420). We also use a low chopping frequency of 24 HZ. The transient photovoltage (TPV) equipment was analyzed on a self-assembly apparatus. A laser radiation pulse (wavelength of 355 nm and pulse width of 5 ns) from a third-harmonic Nd:YAG laser (Polaris II, New Wave Research, Inc) was used to excite the products. The TPV signal was recorded by the 500 MHz digital phosphor oscilloscope (TDS 5054, Tektronix). A Jobin Yvon FluoroMax-4 was used to analyze the Photoluminescence (PL) spectra with an excitation wavelength of 340 nm. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were carried out on a Thermo VG Scientific ESCALAB 250 spectrometer using monochromatized Al-Kα excitation. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra of N-TiO2 and CDs/N-TiO2 nanocomposites were analyzed on a Fourier transform-infrared radiation spectrophotometer (SHIMADZU 1.50SU1). Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurements were performed by using an electrochemistry workstation (CHI 630B, made in China) at 0.8 V bias potential and 20 mV of amplitude under dark condition.

For each test, We added 30 mg of the photocatalyst into 20 mL of RhB (10 mg/L) settled solution. To disperse the photocatalyst evenly, we sonicated the mixture for 5 min. Then the adsorption-desorption equilibrium was reached after stirring for 30 min in the absence of light. When all the above steps were completed, the quartz beaker was placed under the visible light (Wavelength>420 nm). We extracted 1 mL of solid-liquid mixture from the quartz beaker per one minute during the experimental process. A UV–vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, UV-3600) was used to determine the concentration of residual RhB after centrifuging.

The photodegradation procedure that we carried out to photodegrade the Cr(VI) was similar with that of RhB photodegradation, in which Cr(VI) solution (K2Cr2O7 20 mg/L) replaced the RhB solution. A UV–vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, UV-3600) was used to measure the amounts of Cr(VI) after centrifuging. The λmax of absorbance was about 350 nm (Fig. 1 ).

The XRD analysis of several important photocatalysts were depicted in Fig. 2 . For the all above photocatalysts, the major diffraction peaks were found at 2θ = 25.3°, 37.8°, 48.0°, 53.9°,55.1°, 62.7°, and 75.0°, which originated from the (101), (004), (200), (105), (211), (204), and (215) planes of anatase (JCPDSNo. 21-1272). When compared modified catalysts with TiO2, significant shifts of XRD peaks didn’t appear, indicating that doping of N into the TiO2 didn’t influence crystal phase and structure of the nanocomposites [34]. Meanwhile, there were not characteristic peaks of CDs in all the nanocomposites that contains CDs, because the amount of CDs was too less in these nanocomposites [35].

The SEM images of several important nanocomposites were exhibited in Fig. 3 (A–C), respectively. The TiO2, N-TiO2 and 30CDs/N-TiO2 nanoparticles also exhibited uniform morphologies with diameters of about 10 nm. The form of the 30CDs/N-TiO2 was researched by TEM (Fig. 3(D–F)). Fig. 3(D) showed the TEM result of 30CDs/N-TiO2, the particle diameter of nanocomposite was about 10 nm. The HRTEM images of 30CDs/N-TiO2 were showed in Fig. 3(E) and (F), affirm that the combination of CDs and N-TiO2 have been achieved. The 0.34 nm measured lattice fringe spacing originates from (101) plane of the anatase reported before [36]. The lattice width approximately 0.24 nm originates from (100) plane of the graphene carbon [37].

The FTIR spectra of two main nanocomposites were exhibited in Fig. 4 . For two nanocomposites, two likely wide peaks of TiO and TiOTi bonds of titanium dioxide are at 800−400 cm−1 [38]. When compared with NTiO2, a different peak that belongs to OH stretching vibration appears at 3478.1 cm−1 in 30CDs/N-TiO2, which might come from hydrogen peroxide on the surface of nanomaterials [39]. For the same situation, the peak at 1638.8 cm−1 belongs to CC stretching vibration, which come from the CDs in 30CDs/N-TiO2 [40].

The chemical constituents of 30CDs/N-TiO2 and the mutual effect between elements was characterized by XPS (Fig. 5 ). There are titanium (Ti2p), nitrogen (N1 s), carbon (C1 s) and oxygen (O1 s) in this nanocomposite (Fig. 5(A)). The peaks of 458.8 and 464.5 eV originate from Ti (2p3/2) and Ti (2p1/2) (Fig. 5(B)) [41]. In the O1 s XPS spectrum, the peak of TiO bond is at 530.1 eV and an obvious peak that originates from CO appears at 531.1 eV, ulteriorly revealing the exsistence of the CDs (Fig. 5(C)) [42,34]. In the N1s XPS spectrum, the peaks appear at 399.5 and 400.2 eV originate from OTiN and TiON bonds and this result indicates that the N was successfully doped into TiO2 (Fig. 5(D)) [33]. In C1s XPS spectrum, we discovered the characteristic peaks of CC/CC (graphite), CO and CO bonds, which were at 284.7, 285.6, and 288.8 eV, respectively (Fig. 5(E)) [43,44]. These manifest the presence of CDs, which was coincident with the TEM and FTIR results.

There are the UV–vis DRS of all the photocatalysts (Fig. 6 (A)). The TiO2 presented an absorption edge at ca. 419.7 nm, we did not discover an obvious absorption band in visible-light region, implying that it could not absorb visible light. Once N was introduced into the TiO2 lattice, the absorption edge showed a obvious red-shift from 419.7 to 439.9 nm compared with the TiO2, and we discovered a strong absorption band in visible-light region, implying that the optical responsive range was broadened to visible light by doping of N. However, loading of CDs on the TiO2 or N-TiO2 didn’t have obvious red -shift to visible light. By using Tauc approach, We discovered that the band gap energy (Eg) of the TiO2 that our prepared before was 3.19 eV, which is nearly consistent with Eg of anatase reported before (Fig.6(B)) [45]. Besides, we found the Eg of the N-TiO2 was 3.07 eV, which was narrower than that of the TiO2 (Fig.6(B)). So the extra charge carriers can be easily formed in N-TiO2 under visible light [46].

BET tests of two main nanocomposites are shown in Fig. 7 . The specific surface areas of N-TiO2 and 30CDs/N-TiO2 were measured as 139.08 m2 g−1 and 153.77 m2 g−1, respectively. As a contrast, the 30CDs/N-TiO2 sample showed a little higher given superficial area. Loading of CDs on N-TiO2 nanocomposite might lead to this result [47].

Thereafter, we all knew that the decreasing charge carrier recombination rate was matched with lower PL intensity, implying the higher photocatalytic performance [48,49]. As shown in Fig. 8 , the PL intensity of the N-TiO2 was almost in coincident with the TiO2, which manifested that the N-doping did not influence the charge carrier recombination of TiO2. When we combined N-TiO2 with CDs, the PL intensity decreased observably, revealing that the electrons could be transfered more easily and the charge carrier recombination rate decreased in xCDs/N-TiO2 [50]. Furthermore, the peak at 467 nm of the 30CDs/N-TiO2 was observably weaker than that of the composites, which implied that the 30CDs/N-TiO2 had the lowest charge carrier recombination rate in the composites.

We all knew that the higher SPV response intensity corresponds to the higher separation efficiency of charge carriers [51]. The positive intensity indicates that the photocatalyst is an n-type semiconductor, while the negative intensity manifests that the sample shows p-type conducting character [52]. As showed in Fig. 9 , with the doping of N into the TiO2, the SPV response transform positive intensity into negative, which implying the N doping let n-type TiO2 show p-type conducting character that makes it easier to transfer electrons to the CDs [26]. Meanwhile, when the surface of N-TiO2 was loaded with CDs, the SPV signal increased markedly, indicating that loading of CDs on the nanocomposite can notably promote the separation efficiency. The charge carriers separation efficiency of all the nanocomposites followed following sequence:30CDs/N-TiO2>50CDs/N-TiO2>
10CDs/N-TiO2>N-TiO2. This result corresponds to the PL result that CDs have the ability to promote the separation efficiency of the nanocomposites.

The transient photovoltage response (Fig. 10 ) was ulterior applied to characterize the dynamic performance of light-induced electron-hole pairs action, the excitation wavelength was 355 nm [53]. The positive value of pure TiO2 was on behalf of the n-type semiconductor. On the contrary, the negative value of N-TiO2 and xCDs/N-TiO2 photocatalysts signal indicated that these photocatalysts show p-type conducting character [54], which was in good agreement with SPV resultes. We can see the transient photovoltage signal of all nanocomposites instantly, demonstrating that lightinduced electron-hole pairs could divide fleetly. The enhanced transient photovoltage signals of xCDs/N-TiO2 could be seen by contrast with N-TiO2, implying that the lightinduced charge carriers could transfer more easily after loading of CDs on N-TiO2 [55]. However, the TiO2 and N-TiO2 achieved the maximum value rapidly, relating to the outstanding separation efficiency within a short space time [49].However, a decreased photovoltage intensity was discovered simultaneously, compared to the xCDs/N-TiO2, implying the recombination time of light-induced charge carriers of TiO2 and N-TiO2 were observably shortened [56]. That was the cause for the observably poor photodegradation of TiO2 and N-TiO2. The transient photovoltage intensity followed the sequence：30CDs/N-TiO2>50CDs/N-TiO2>

10CDs/N-TiO2>N-TiO2. The above result well fits the PL and SPV results. For this phenomenon, we guessed that CDs could decrease lightinduced charge carriers recombination rate and increase the recombination time.

We choosed Rhodamine B (RhB) to test photocatalytic performance of all the nanocomposites that we prepared before. The change of RhB concentration could be ignored within 5 min under visible light when we didn’t add any catalysts into the RhB solution (Fig. 11 (A)). When pure TiO2 and CDs were added into RhB solution, the changes of RhB concentration could also be neglected, severally. And when the N-TiO2, the mixture of N-TiO2 and CDs and CDs/TiO2 composites were added into the solution, 19.97 %, 10.87 % and 47.06 % of RhB were removed within 5 min under visible light, respectively. But when the 30CDs/N-TiO2 composite existed, RhB was almost completely removed.

We discovered that the kinetics of RhB photodegradation of all the photocatalysts that we prepared before (Fig. 11(B)) were accorded with the pseudo-first-order rate formula (Eq. (1)):
(1) ln(Ct/C0)=-k t
where C0 is the original concentration of RhB solution and Ct is the concentration of RhB solution that has been photodegradated for t minutes, and k is the apparent reaction rate constant. The lines can be well fitted linearly (R2≥0.80), showing that the data fits the pseudo-first-order kinetic model highly. As a result, values of the rate constant for RhB photodegradation by various samples were showed in Fig. 11(B). It was seen that the apparent rate constant for RhB photodegradation by the 30CDs/N-TiO2 compound was up to 1.0341 min−1, which was about 10.17-,25.40-,48.20-,85.52-, and 197.88-fold faster than that of CDs/TiO2, N-TiO2, CDs + N-TiO2(mixture of CDs and N-TiO2), pure TiO2 and CDs, severally.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of CDs loading amounts on photocatalytic performance of CDs/N-TiO2
ce:section.ce:para: It is clear that loading of CDs on the N-TiO2 dramatically promoted the photodegradation rate (Fig. 11(C)). And 94.3 % of RhB was removed when the surface of N-TiO2 loaded with a small amount of CDs(10CDs/N-TiO2). The photodegradation rates of RhB was improved from 0.5004 to 1.0844 min−1 with the addition of CDs from 10CDs/N-TiO2 to 30CDs/N-TiO2. However, further adding the CDs contents from 30CDs/N-TiO2 to 50CDs/N-TiO2 reduced the photodegradation rates of RhB from 1.0844 to 0.5448 min−1 (Fig. 11(D)). Because the high CDs amount might contend for the photons with N-TiO2, So the photodegradation rates decreased [57].

Cycling experiments were carried out to research into the consistence of the nanocomposite (Fig. 11(E)). The RhB photodegradation rate still maintained at ca. 94.1 % after five recycling experiments, revealing the 30CDs/N-TiO2 compound showed the excellent textural consistence.

As observed in Fig. 11(F), the photodegradation of RhB by 30CDs/N-TiO2 fairly relied on the photocatalyst dosage. The RhB was barely photodegradated under visible light irradiation for 5 min when we didn’t add nanocomposite. However, the RhB photodegradation rate markedly increased from 5.2 % to 62.3 % after adding 1 g/L of the nanocomposite. Besides, a remarkable enhancement was achieved when photocatalyst dosage was increased more, and the photodegradation rates were 68.7 % and 99.8 % when photocatalyst dosages were increased to 2 g/L and 3 g/L, severally. However, the photodegradation rate decreased from 99.8 % to 95.4 % when the photocatalyst dosage was increased from 3 g/L to 4 g/L. More light-induced electrons and holes were produced by adding more photocatalyst dosage, which ultimately enhanced the photodegradation of RhB. However, when the photocatalyst dosage was too much, the transmittance of suspension would be reduced [32] (Tables 1 and 2
).

In the representative photodegradation process of dyestuff, light-induced holes (h+), superoxide radicals (
O2 −) and hydroxyl radicals (
OH) are three dominating active substances [61]. Various scavenger tests were put into effect on 30CDs/N-TiO2 to make clear the mechanisms of photocatalytic process. The different inhibiting effects in the scavenger tests were showed when we added EDTA, BQ, and IPA, respectively (Fig. 12 (A)), whereas addition of IPA resulted in a mild(<9 %) inhibiting effect on the photodegradation of RhB. However, the obvious inhibition rates of 43.6 %, and 56.1 % were achieved when the EDTA and BQ were in the presence of the RhB solution, severally. As a result, the h+, and
O2- are more crucial than
OH in the photodegradation process (Fig. 12(B)).

Because of these outcomes, the schematic diagram of light-induced electron-hole pairs and free radicals generated in CDs/N-TiO2 photocatalysts with the feasible photodegradation mechanism was demonstrated in Fig.13 . For one thing, the doping of N into TiO2 can let TiO2 respond to visible light promote the and make it easier to transfer electrons to the CDs [29–32], as proved by UV–vis DRS, SPV and TPV. For another thing, the electron-hole pairs separation efficiency can be enhanced and lightinduced electrons can be captured by CDs when CDs plays a role of an electron reservoir, as proved by PL, SPV and TPV measurements. The following equations show the mechanism of the photocatalytic process was shown in the following equations:
(1) CDs/N-TiO2+hv = e−(CDs)+h+(N-TiO2)
H2O + h+ =
OH+H+ F
(3) O2+e− =
O2-
(4) h+ +RhB = CO2+H2O
(5)
OH + RhB = CO2+H2O
(6)
O2 −+RhB = CO2+H2O

Original energy band structure of N-TiO2 was provided to understand why CDs/N-TiO2 photocatalyst have a dramatical performance when compared with other photocatalysts. The atom's Mulliken electronegativity definition (Eqs. (2) and (3)) was used to theoretically compute the band position of N-TiO2 [62]:
(2) EVB= X – Ec + 0.5Eg
(3) ECB = EVB − Eg

where EVB is the valence band potential, ECB is the conduction band potential, Ec is the energy of free electrons on the hydrogen scale (4.5 eV), Eg is the band gap energy of the photocatalyst, X is the electronegativity of the photocatalyst that can be calculated from electronegativity of constituent atoms [63]. The Eg of N-TiO2 obtained from Fig. 6(B) is 3.07 eV. The X of N-TiO2 was theoretically computed to be 5.81. So the EVB and ECB of N-TiO2 were 2.85 eV and −0.22 eV. Usually, these light-induced charge carriers recombine fleetly and then only few electron-hole pairs left which join in the photodegradation. However, due to the properties of CDs and p-type conducting character of N-TiO2, which made it easier to transfer electrons to the CDs, this undesirable recombination process didn't occur. The electrons would generate from the N-TiO2 and then shifted into CDs when the CDs/N-TiO2 photocatalysts respond to visible light. At last, superoxide free radicals (
O2 −) formed when the O2 captured the electrons. At the same time, H2O was oxidized to develop
OH by the holes that also can participate in the dyestuff degradation immediately.

In Fig.14 , We compared the 30CDs/N-TiO2 with the CDs and other TiO2-based catalysts by photodegradation of Cr(VI) under simulated solar light. The concentration of Cr(VI) hardly reduced after irradiation for 4 min when we added the TiO2 nanocomposite into the solution. However, because of the desorption from catalyst by stirring, the concentration of Cr(VI) slightly increased. On the contrary, CDs/TiO2, N-TiO2, CDs + N-TiO2(mixture of CDs and N-TiO2) showed a mild photoreduction behavior, leading to 12.1 %, 71 % and 87.8 % of Cr(VI) reduction ratios, severally. However, when the 30CDs/N-TiO2 composite existed, Cr(VI) was almost completely reduced (Fig.14(A)). Obviously, the photoreduction ratios were observably increased from 71 % to 95.5 % and 99.8 % when the CDs loading level was improved from N-TiO2 to 10CDs/N-TiO2 and 30CDs/N-TiO2, severally. Because the electrons can transfer to the surface of photocatalysts more easily when added the CDs on the surface of N-TiO2. However, with the more amount of CDs added (30CDs/N-TiO2 to 50CDs/N-TiO2), the Cr(VI) photoreduction efficiency decreased (Fig. 14(B)) for the following two reasons: (1) The adsorption of simulated solar light was competed by the excess CDs amount and N-TiO2; (2) The charge carriers recombinated in the overmuch CDs [33].

In summary, we prepared the CDs/N-TiO2 nanocomposite that has dramatic performance by mechanically mixing carbon quantum dots with N-TiO2 instead of the conventional calcination or solvothermal step, and the N-TiO2 showed p-type conducting character. Usually, p-type conducting character was rarely found when TiO2 was doped and the photodegradation time of same concentrations of pollutants under visible light or solar light always needs tens of minutes according to previous reports. But when we compared 30CDs/N-TiO2 with TiO2 and N-TiO2, our synthesized nanocomposite showed prominent photocatalytic performance on RhB photodegradation. The RhB (10 mg/L) photodegradation can be accomplished in 5 min totally. The most important effect was played by h+ in three active species when we photodegradated RhB. The doping of N into TiO2 can let TiO2 respond to visible light and show p-type conducting character that makes it easier to transfer electrons to CDs. CDs can act as electrons reservoir that can enhance electron-hole pairs separation efficiency of the photocatalyst. Besides, the Cr(VI) (20 mg/L) photoreduction can be achieved in 4 min completely under solar light when we added the 30CDs/N-TiO2 nanocomposite into it, showing a broad prospect for practical use of CDs/N-TiO2 nanocomposite.

Jinteng Zhang: Formal analysis, Investigation, Writing - original draft, Methodology. Yuehong Cao: Data curation. Peng Zhao: Data curation. Tengfeng Xie: Funding acquisition. Yanhong Lin: Conceptualization, Validation, Resources, Writing - review & editing, Supervision, Project administration. Zhao Mu: Funding acquisition.
